entries.title: The Art of the Giveaway
entries.summary: The Trump administration is giving Saudi Arabia exactly what it wants. For what?
entries.content:
The Trump administration is giving Saudi Arabia exactly what it wants. For what?

What kind of negotiator gives the other side everything that it wants with the hope of eventually getting something in return?

It’s a question that even some of President Trump’s own allies are asking after his administration signed a landmark deal to develop a civilian nuclear program in Saudi Arabia using American technology. The deal’s opponents, in the United States and Israel, worry that the new pact might ultimately allow for uranium enrichment—and if so, that Trump just gave Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman the tools he needs to build a bomb. What did the U.S. require in return? Pretty much zip.

That, at least, is how things stood when the agreement was finalized yesterday. Then, just before 8 a.m. today, Trump took to social media to deliver what seemed like an after-the-fact ultimatum: The deal, he wrote, is “totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords,” the Middle East initiative launched in Trump’s first term that is designed to normalize relations between Israel and countries in the region.

Trump-administration officials argued that the deal is an “America First” policy triumph because the 30-year, multibillion-dollar agreement gives American companies (which already do huge business in Saudi Arabia) a central role in developing the kingdom’s nuclear infrastructure while shutting out foreign competitors. Karoline Leavitt, the White House press secretary, told reporters today that the arrangement “will ultimately benefit American industry workers and supply chains.”

It may not benefit Trump’s image as a consummate dealmaker, however. The framework of Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham accords and, in return, getting defense guarantees and U.S. help to develop a civilian nuclear program has been on the table for years. It came close to fruition during the Biden administration. But the kingdom received a defense package in November and the nuclear deal this week. Neither agreement stipulated the normalizing of relations with Israel as a precondition. “Leave it to this administration to negotiate after the signatures are barely dry,” Elisa Ewers, a senior fellow for Middle East studies at the Council on Foreign Relations, told us.

When the agreement was unveiled yesterday, U.S. lawmakers and Israeli politicians were startled by its lax conditions for Riyadh. Trump by this morning may have decided to provide reassurance with his Abraham Accords announcement—so he did what he often does.

“The president wakes up every day, and every day, at least on Middle East policy, is an improvisation,” F. Gregory Gause, a professor emeritus at the Bush School of Government and Public Service at Texas A&M University, told us. “He was getting some pushback on the Saudi deal, and so he added this sweetener to it for people in Congress who might feel that if the Saudis had a regular diplomatic relationship with Israel, they would be less concerned.”

Trump, he noted, may improvise again and remove the stipulation if the furor dies down. Last week, the president said that the U.S. would impose a 20 percent fee on the value of the cargo on ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz to counter Iranian efforts to control the waterway. The next day, the president reversed himself. “So it could be that by tomorrow, this goes away from the president’s mind,” Gause said. But if he gives the Saudis what they want without asking for anything in return, he also would have thrown away all of his leverage.

In 2023, top officials in the Biden administration shuttled between Washington and Riyadh, believing that they were on the precipice of a major breakthrough: Bin Salman, known as MBS, was close to joining the Abraham Accords, a development that officials saw as a bulwark against Iran’s rising nuclear threat in the region. Talks to flesh out the details were scheduled for mid-October of that year. But the negotiations unraveled after Hamas’s October 7 attack on Israel and the devastating Gaza war that followed. In the aftermath, Saudi leaders concluded that normalization was politically untenable.

Biden-administration officials then looked for other ways to entice the Saudis to both restart normalization talks and help secure the Gaza Strip. They focused on a mutual-defense agreement, which included nuclear cooperation, even though President Biden had made known his dislike for the crown prince after the 2018 murder of the journalist Jamal Khashoggi. Jon Wolfsthal, a former adviser on U.S.-Saudi nuclear cooperation, told us that the Biden administration had calculated that “if the U.S. didn’t get in the game, particularly as a nuclear provider, one of America’s adversaries might, and that is much worse for U.S. national security.”

But with the 2024 election looming, Saudi Arabia’s government gambled that a Trump victory would bring more favorable terms, and the talks stalled.

The Saudis were right. Saudi leaders already saw in Trump a partner whose approach was similar to their own: pragmatic, transactional, and largely unbothered by concerns about human rights. The relationship extended beyond official diplomacy. MBS had developed a close rapport with Trump’s son-in-law Jared Kushner during Trump’s first term, communicating with him directly over WhatsApp. Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund had previously committed $2 billion to Kushner’s private-equity firm.

Following his return to the Oval Office, Trump grew fixated on getting Saudi Arabia to normalize ties with Israel. As in his first term, Trump picked Saudi Arabia for his first official overseas visit, arriving with an entourage of American business executives in an effort to foster deeper corporate ties, particularly in technology. During a meeting in Washington, D.C., in November, Trump, raising his voice, pressed MBS to join the Abraham Accords. MBS insisted that the only way to achieve this would be by agreeing on a path toward Palestinian statehood. (Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has rejected the prospect of a sovereign Palestinian state.) Still, the U.S. and Saudi Arabia reached an understanding on a defense agreement, which would include the sale of advanced F-35 fighter jets.

Months later, Trump tried to link an expansion of the Abraham Accords to his plan for peace with Iran, suggesting in a conference call with several regional leaders that they sign up, according to U.S. and Middle Eastern officials. After that conversation finished, he called MBS personally to press the issue once more. But Trump got the same answer. Still, talks over a Saudi civilian nuclear program continued.

The Trump administration says that this week’s deal is designed to help Saudi Arabia develop civilian nuclear power only. Energy Secretary Chris Wright, who signed the agreement, said in a statement that the terms “uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation.” The Saudi government said that the deal supports its domestic efforts to diversify energy sources, and that the government is committed to the “highest international standards” of nuclear safety, security, and nonproliferation.

But Saudi Arabia has also talked about becoming a nuclear-weapons power if Iran were able to develop its own weapon. Preventing Tehran from doing so is a central goal of the U.S. conflict, but its outcome is uncertain, as is the fate of Iran’s nuclear program. “Saudi Arabia does not want to acquire any nuclear bomb, but without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible,” MBS told CBS News in 2018.

Advocates of nonproliferation contend that the U.S.-Saudi deal lacks the necessary guardrails to prevent that: no measures to prevent the sharing of uranium-enrichment and plutonium-separation technologies, no commitment by Saudi Arabia to comply with the safety standards of, and monitoring by, the International Atomic Energy Agency.

Under the agreement, American companies would build nuclear reactors in Saudi Arabia, and the two countries would study whether Saudi Arabia should eventually enrich uranium on its own instead of importing nuclear fuel. Enriching uranium is necessary to produce the fuel for nuclear-power plants but can also be a key step toward making a nuclear weapon. “Your best route to avoiding proliferation is this kind of very, very close relationship with the United States, with U.S. technology, that we control, that we monitor, and that is of a long duration,” Victoria Coates, a former deputy national security adviser to Trump, who now works at the Heritage Foundation, told us.

Trump denied that enrichment is on the table. The deal notes that the U.S. will ultimately decide, after a study is conducted, whether enrichment shouldn’t take place, and if that’s the conclusion, Saudi Arabia would be barred from pursuing it on its own or with another country for 10 years. But giving the U.S., rather than international inspectors, that authority could lead other regimes to choose their own inspectors too, opening the door to broader proliferation.

“Making all of this subjective—and ‘you can choose your own inspectors,’ and all those other things—is a really bad route to go down,” Jon Alterman, a global-security scholar at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, told us. In theory, he said, the agreement could give Iranians the license and precedent to credibly say: “We have Russian inspectors, we have Chinese inspectors, so we’re doing just what the Saudis are doing.”

Alterman noted that the U.S. even has a model for a better arrangement: a 2009 agreement with the United Arab Emirates. “The Emiratis built a genuinely robust and safe domestic nuclear program,” Alterman said. “There are robust safeguards against proliferation. And it deepened the partnership between the UAE and the United States.” But that’s not what the U.S. asked of Saudi Arabia this week.

entries.title: North Carolina’s Threat to Legal Assistance for the Poor
entries.summary: Republicans in the state legislature abruptly slashed funding for civil legal aid for low-income clients this month.
entries.content:
Republicans in the state legislature abruptly slashed funding for civil legal aid for low-income clients this month.

This is an edition of The Atlantic Daily, a newsletter that guides you through the biggest stories of the day, helps you discover new ideas, and recommends the best in culture. Sign up for it here.

When Hurricane Helene hit western North Carolina in September 2024, it left behind it a trail of death and destruction—and a huge number of legal headaches. Residents struggled to understand the process of receiving help from FEMA, people were beset by scams and price gouging, and tenants whose homes had been destroyed or made uninhabitable puzzled over their rights.

Pisgah Legal Services, a nonprofit that provides civil legal aid in the region, had 120 staffers, and all of them were somehow affected, Jaclyn Kiger, Pisgah’s executive director, told me. Within about a week, though, Pisgah Legal had hosted three clinics; it ultimately helped thousands of Helene victims.

Civil legal services like these may not be well understood, but they serve an essential role in ensuring that everyone has access to justice. The Supreme Court’s landmark 1963 decision in Gideon v. Wainwright established that any criminal defendant has a right to legal counsel. But most legal issues are civil, not criminal. This includes any number of disputes between parties, but also issues such as evictions, scams targeting senior citizens, and domestic-violence protective orders. Recourse to courts in such cases can be lifesaving, but they are not free, and they are not easy for ordinary people to handle on their own.

Since 1981, every state has created programs to fund civil legal services with Interest on Lawyers’ Trust Accounts. It’s an elegant solution: Lawyers frequently hold money in accounts for clients, accruing interest that doesn’t truly belong to any party. IOLTA-funded programs use that interest to provide legal support without any cost to taxpayers. Kiger told me that Pisgah Legal received nearly $2 million in fiscal year 2025; overall, NC IOLTA provided clients roughly $12 million for civil legal services that year.

But in a budget passed this month, the Republican-dominated North Carolina General Assembly effectively cut this funding off. The budget permits $2 million in IOLTA funding in the coming fiscal year for civil legal aid, but that will sunset in the future. (Governor Josh Stein, a Democrat, signed the budget—the first that the state has managed to pass in more than a year—while criticizing elements of it, including the IOLTA cut, which he said “cruelly punishes low-income people who need access to justice.”) Most IOLTA money will be redirected to criminal legal services for indigent defendants, which the state is already constitutionally required to fund. Providers I’ve spoken with recently are crestfallen but also baffled. Although they all support funding for public defenders, they note that civil legal aid gets no other state money. “Hundreds of thousands of low-income people in the mountains are going to suffer,” Kiger said.

Civil legal services have historically been a bipartisan priority, popular among both parties and lawyers as well. Last year, a coalition of leaders of some of the state’s biggest law firms wrote a letter to the state supreme court backing IOLTA funding for civil legal services. “We believe a fair and functioning court system is not a partisan goal—it is a constitutional one,” they wrote. “By enabling litigants to participate effectively, IOLTA-funded legal aid strengthens both the efficiency and fairness of judicial proceedings.”

But some North Carolina Republicans have objected to the fact that some of the state’s biggest providers of civil legal aid also engage in progressive political advocacy or do legal work related to immigration or social-justice issues. The driving force behind the change in this month’s budget is state House Speaker Destin Hall, who is also an attorney in private practice. “IOLTA funds were shifted to ensure they’re not being utilized to bankroll progressive political pet projects like the NC Justice Center or provide government services to illegal immigrants. Instead, those funds will be used to provide legal services to underserved communities,” he said in a statement in June.

To that end, the general assembly placed political limits on a small portion of civil legal aid. The budget allows IOLTA money collected before July 2025 to be distributed, but only $2 million a year until the funds run out, and only to providers who don’t engage in political advocacy or take cases involving transgender health care or federal immigration law. This is less a way of removing politics from legal aid and more a way of instituting a different set of ideological priorities, and in any case, it applies only to this small and disappearing pot of money. The overarching goal appears to be not just going after progressive groups, but ensuring reductions in civil legal aid per se. This has led providers to speculate that the general assembly is less worried about “wokeness” and more interested in defending business interests. “I think it comes down to who we serve,” Kiger said. “We are serving the most vulnerable among us.”

When I contacted Hall’s office to try to understand the decision, a spokesperson asked me for detailed questions but never replied when I sent them. Ashley Campbell, who leads Legal Aid of North Carolina, the state’s largest civil-legal-aid firm, told me that she sought meetings with Hall but was rebuffed. In a statement, the executive director of the North Carolina State Bar lamented that “months of good-faith efforts” by the bar and NC IOLTA leaders had failed to sway the general assembly: “This loss of funding will prove devastating to the tens of thousands of low-income North Carolinians facing legal concerns that threaten their housing stability, income, access to health care and benefits, and safety from domestic violence and human trafficking.”

The defunding of IOLTA in North Carolina is a major event in the state, but it could have national implications. As I have been reporting for more than a decade now, many of the major currents in national politics have originated in the Old North State. The state has also been used as a proving ground, including for Donald Trump–style attacks on elections. Right now, the partisan warfare over civil legal aid in North Carolina is an outlier; Texas GOP legislators even strengthened IOLTA funding in 2023. But a failed Montana bill last year tried to weaken IOLTA, and Trump has proposed eliminating or drastically reducing funding for the Legal Services Corporation, a national civil-legal-aid group established by Congress. People I spoke with in North Carolina worry that the state budget could provide a template for other states as well. If that happens, access to justice could become just another luxury good.

Related:

Here are three new stories from The Atlantic:

Today’s News

  • President Trump said the nuclear agreement signed with Saudi Arabia yesterday is contingent on the kingdom normalizing relations with Israelby joining the Abraham Accords, a condition that had not been publicly disclosed when the deal was announced.
  • A member of Vice President Vance’s Secret Service detail is under administrative investigationand may face a criminal inquiry over allegations of compromising operations and information security. The Secret Service has not disclosed what information was allegedly compromised.
  • Global oil prices surpassed $100 a barrelfor the first time since May, after Houthi militants claimed to have struck two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea.

Dispatches

  • Time-Travel Thursdays: Jake Lundberg explores what an office murder in 1841revealed about the American workplace.

Explore all of our newsletters here.

Evening Read

The New Chilling Effect

By Jacob Blane, Annabelle Garland and Sam Gross

If you’re looking for a reason to despair over the state of American education, there are many places you could start. You could, for example, worry about the all-consuming phones—or the gambling and TikTok addictions that come with them. You could worry about the

free fall in literacy, the decline in math fluency, the rise of AI, the insanity of college-obsessed parents, the sharp increase in truancy, the teen mental-health crisis, and on and on.

Yet something that deserves more attention is damage to free expression—not in the classroom but in the public sphere of high school: the realms of student newspapers, student government, and other aspects of everyday life. School is meant to prepare students for the real world, but too many institutions are training kids to be silent.

More From The Atlantic

Culture Break

Watch with everyone else. The Odyssey’s box-office success affirms the power of spectacle, David Sims writes.

Don’t just take the iPad away. In 2014, Daniel Willingham gave advice on how to trick your kids into reading all summer long.

PS

Katie Crutchfield, who records as Waxahatchee, is one of the best active singer-songwriters; her 2024 album, Tigers Blood, was one of my favorites of this decade. We probably can’t expect new music from her too soon, because she just had a baby, but two of her sometime collaborators have new records coming out that I’m hoping will tide me over during her absence. Brennan Wedl’s self-titled album (co-produced by Crutchfield) is out next month, and yesterday she released a rerecording of her excellent song “Two Dollar Pistol.” Jess Williamson, Crutchfield’s partner in the sublime one-off band Plains, is releasing A Mile South of Heaven in October, and the lead single, “Goodbye to All That,” is a promising tease.

— David

Rafaela Jinich contributed to this newsletter.

entries.title: The War Is Lost. Now Fix What It Broke.
entries.summary: America has to rethink how it moves resources, fights wars, and deters adversaries.
entries.content:
America has to rethink how it moves resources, fights wars, and deters adversaries.

The sooner we acknowledge that the United States has lost the war with Iran and stop fighting, the better. Because we now have a lot of work to do.

On Tuesday, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth was pressed to explain before the Senate what the United States had accomplished in its war with Iran. Sputtering with fury at times, he claimed that Washington had won “a historic military victory.” But even if that were so, as a certain dead Prussian once counseled, nations do not fight wars to achieve military objectives. They fight wars to achieve political objectives.

The United States has not secured any of its political objectives in the war with Iran, and indeed, it has produced a situation in July that is markedly worse than the one antebellum: Iran’s former supreme leader, an elderly man known for his extreme views but also his caution, has been replaced by a group of leaders who are, if anything, more extreme and far less cautious about confronting the United States and its partners in the region.

The Strait of Hormuz, which was open prior to the start of the conflict, is now largely closed. And the Arabian Peninsula’s other choke point, the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, appears to have been shut by Iran’s partners in Yemen, the Houthis. In the spring, American energy executives sounded like Chicken Little as they warned of oil and gas shortages that a reduction in Chinese oil imports helped forestall. But Exxon and Chevron have more recently cautioned that an energy crunch really is inevitable if the situation does not improve, and that this could threaten the entire global economy.

The U.S. does not have a workable military solution to this problem. Iran can threaten tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz with little more than inexpensive drones. And, thanks to this conflict, Tehran has discovered that it has powerful psychological leverage over shipping companies and their insurers—so much so that it hardly needs to lay mines to block the strait.

For the U.S. to finance this war beyond the $37.5 billion that the Pentagon admits it has already spent—the actual number is surely much higher—would be to throw good money after bad. The Trump administration needs to face up to having lost so that it can focus on preventing the effects of an embarrassing but ultimately manageable defeat from spreading beyond the Persian Gulf. This will involve rethinking some fundamentals—such as how the region’s oil reaches world markets, how the United States wages war, and how Iran can be deterred from making trouble for American interests in the future.

The Strait of Hormuz is now, and likely indefinitely will be, under a degree of Iranian control that did not exist prior to the conflict. Washington and its allies, together with private-sector investors, will need to devote considerable time and money toward securing alternate routes for Middle Eastern oil to reach global markets. Some infrastructure already exists. The East-West Pipeline that runs across Saudi Arabia was built during the Iran-Iraq War, which was the last time oil tankers struggled to navigate the strait. This pipeline moves roughly 7 million barrels of oil a day. The Saudis are considering doubling that capacity. The United Arab Emirates has a similar pipeline that bypasses the strait, and whose volume will also likely need to double. Oil and gas pipelines, like other oil infrastructure in the Gulf, are vulnerable to Iranian drone strikes, but they are relatively easy to repair.

The United States should encourage the Saudis and Emiratis to run branches of their pipelines through Yemen and Oman, to keep those countries invested in maintaining the free flow of oil and gas out of the peninsula. Chevron is apparently speaking with Iraq about a new pipeline—through Syria, no less—to move that country’s oil to market. The sooner these conduits are built, the better. (Also, just a friendly suggestion, but this maybe wasn’t the best month to crow about ending federal subsidies for wind- and solar-power generation.)

The Iran war has brought a few things into focus—among them, that the United States needs to rethink its approach to warfare in the region and beyond. For all of the time that the U.S. Central Command undoubtedly spent planning and rehearsing for a confrontation with Iran, the conduct of this war betrays a certain failure of imagination that has cost the U.S. military a great deal. The United States appears to have assumed that its abundance of precision weaponry would give it a huge advantage over the Iranians—which it did, for at least a few weeks. But precision weaponry was not decisive.

Relatively low-cost Iranian missiles and drones have wrought havoc upon U.S. bases and other targets in the Middle East. American planners should have anticipated as much: Ukrainian drones have been inflicting devastating damage on Russian military assets for several years now, demonstrating just how quickly the nature of warfare has changed. Yet American troops are getting killed in their barracks by cheap Iranian missiles and drones, and their leaders haven’t thought through adequate countermeasures.

This failure belongs not only to the Trump administration but also to the military services and their leaders. The Iran war is almost certainly the easiest drone war that America will ever fight. Countering this cheap and widely accessible technology will only get harder. The United States has to dramatically rethink its posture in the Middle East and around the globe until it figures out how to contend with that.

Finally, Washington will need some fresh ideas if it is to reestablish deterrence with Iran. Prior to last summer, Iran had not made a serious move to stop oil and gas from flowing through the Strait of Hormuz in decades, and likely held back from making rapid advances toward a nuclear weapon, in large part because American military power deterred these actions. But as the American scholar Thomas Schelling noted, the power of a deterrent lies in the threat of its use. Once you actually do the thing you threaten to do, the enemy no longer fears what might happen.

The Trump administration has now squandered not one but two deterrents. Last year’s strikes on Iran’s nuclear facilities did not, as President Trump initially claimed, “obliterate” the country’s nuclear program. I oversaw the contingency planning for such an operation for two years at the Pentagon. The strikes did what we planned them to do: They degraded Iran’s nuclear program, but only temporarily. Likewise, Hegseth is correct that we have done incredible damage to Iran’s conventional military and the industrial base that supports it. But the United States sought above all to deter Iran from doing two things: striking American partners in the region and disrupting maritime traffic. Is Iran now more deterred from taking these actions—or less so?

The answer is clearly the latter. The administration needs either to reach an accord with the Iranians or to establish a credible new deterrent, and fast. The promoters of this war—including The Wall Street Journal’s editorial board and the hawks at various Washington think tanks—have gotten everything they asked for. And it has been a disaster. So now what?

entries.title: The Trump Family Sparks a Revolution in Albania
entries.summary: A luxury development planned by Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump has spurred anti-corruption protests.
entries.content:
A luxury development planned by Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump has spurred anti-corruption protests.

In the summer of 2021, Ivanka Trump and her husband, Jared Kushner, were cruising the Mediterranean by yacht when they stopped for a swim off the Adriatic coast. They splashed over to an island and hiked, barefoot, across its hilly, rugged terrain. “Effectively that’s how we found it,” the president’s eldest daughter would later recount.

The Albanian island that the Trump heirs stumbled on, Robinson Crusoe–like, is called Sazan, once the site of a top-secret military base. Sazan is not undiscovered territory to Albanians; it’s their country’s largest island. Because it was uninhabited for many years, vegetation and wildlife flourished, making it a cherished nature preserve. Luckily for the Trump couple, Albania’s prime minister, Edi Rama, was a guest on the same yacht one of the nights they were on board.

Three years later—and about two months after Donald Trump’s reelection in 2024—an investment committee led by Rama gave preliminary approval to the couple’s plans to develop the island into an opulent resort. The venture extends to nearby land next to a coastal lagoon, which serves as a crucial habitat for flamingos, sea turtles, and other species. The couple’s partners in the project include a pair of Qatari brothers who have financed ultra-luxury developments across the Middle East and South Asia and an Albanian conglomerate with holdings in energy, construction, infrastructure, insurance, and tourism. In a recent podcast interview, Ivanka Trump described the project as “the culmination of all of my experience in real estate, all of my travel, a lot of reflection on how I want to live, how I think people increasingly are wanting to live.”

Many Albanians see matters differently. Hundreds of thousands have joined daily protests against the plans, in one of the country’s largest social mobilizations since the end of Communist rule 35 years ago. The uprising, called the Flamingo Revolution, takes its name from the wild birds that breed in the wetland habitat. Protesters have carried inflatable flamingos and placards with drawings.

The unrest has it all: accusations of organized crime and drug trafficking, competing claims of foreign influence, a cameo by Kanye West. For the participants I spoke with, the demonstrations are about accumulated frustration with Albanian politicians who have failed to give the country a solid economic foundation. “You can interpret it as an anti-Trump demonstration in the sense that it’s opposed to a Trump-like system we see here,” Fatos Lubonja, a writer and dissident who was imprisoned during Albania’s Communist dictatorship, told me: “government decisions made in secret, for the benefit of the few, motivated by money linked to oligarchs.”

The beauty of the landscape discovered by the American president’s daughter “belongs to all Albanians,” Lubonja said. He paraphrased Dostoyevsky: “And beauty will save the world.” But Rama, a former professional artist, won’t hear it. “I can be lectured about many things—about economy, about legal affairs, about agriculture, about health care,” he said in a recent interview. “But there is one thing that nobody can lecture me, which is beauty.”

The Flamingo Revolution seems, at first glance, the unlikeliest of anti-Trump protests. Albania, a nation of fewer than 3 million people, is one of the most pro-American countries in the world, and has been since the United States opposed Albanian partition all the way back in 1920. More recently, Washington backed Albania’s entry into NATO, in 2009, and has upheld its security in a historically volatile region that’s sometimes called the powder keg of Europe.

In the 18 months since Trump returned to power, I have traveled extensively in European countries with some of the strongest postwar ties to the U.S., including Britain, Germany, and Denmark. One of the questions I hear most often is why the Americans opposed to Trump aren’t mounting larger and more sustained demonstrations against him. The question arises especially when I ask European diplomats why the leaders of their countries don’t stand up more forcefully to an American president who insults them and entertains annexing their territory. They turn the inquiry back on me, asking, Why aren’t Americans standing up? Millions have marched in “No Kings” protests; the mobilization in the spring was one of the largest single-day protests in American history. Still, the point remains. Trump made at least $2.2 billion during his first year back in office, as wage growth slowed. He is pursuing an unpopular war, unauthorized by Congress and unsanctioned by international law. Even pro-American Europeans want to know: Why aren’t people in the streets every day?

In Albania, a small country on Europe’s Balkan Peninsula, they are. Today marks the 54th consecutive day of protests triggered by the Trump-family development project. Few would have pegged Albania as the center of the international anti-Trump opposition. But a tour through the country’s recent history suggests that it isn’t as unlikely as it appears.

Albania was once one of the most isolated countries in Europe. It was ruled, from the end of World War II until 1985, by a ruthless and paranoid Communist dictator, Enver Hoxha, whose fear of foreign invasion spawned the construction of an estimated 173,000 bunkers. Albania earned the nickname “Europe’s North Korea.” The Communist state was abolished in 1991, and the anti-Communist opposition swept to power in parliamentary elections a year later. But the transition to a market economy wasn’t easy. Breakneck privatization under a system known as “shock therapy” devastated local industries and drove mass unemployment. In 1997, the collapse of massive, state-sanctioned pyramid schemes wiped out the life savings of about two-thirds of Albanians. A small cadre of politicians has competed for control of the country ever since. Because of its recent experience, the population is highly attuned to signs of autocracy, Dana Fisher, a sociologist at American University who has studied the demographics of the No Kings protests in the U.S., told me. Familiarity can breed complacency. But in this case, it’s inspiring fierce resistance.

The Trump family triggered the uprising, but the target of the protests is Rama. The leader of his country’s Socialist Party, Rama came to power in 2013, as Albania was still sloughing off its insular identity. Albania became a candidate for European Union accession the following year. Rama was once a vehement Trump critic who greeted the prospect of the mogul’s nomination in 2016 by remarking, “God forbid!” He foresaw harm not just to the United States but to the “democratic world.”

Rama is now much more supportive. He hailed Trump’s “remarkable victory” in 2024 and, a year later, echoed the president’s assertions of divine providence, adding, “When Trump says that God saved him to Make America Great Again, he tells only half of the story. The other half is that he saved Trump also to make Europe wake up and get its act together.” Rama is one of the only European leaders to accept an invitation to the Trump-chaired Board of Peace. Domestically, he has sought to improve the Albanian economy by upgrading the hospitality industry. He told The Guardian last year: “We need luxury tourism like a desert needs water.”

That’s in line with Kushner’s ambitions. “I was very, very impressed with his vision for what he wanted to do with Albania,” Trump’s son-in-law told an investment conference. Kushner continued: “I really didn’t know anything about the country. I didn’t understand the history, and the darkness of the history with the Communism, and the terrible leadership actually led to it being fully undeveloped as a nation.”

It’s not Kushner’s first attempt at high-end development in the Balkans. Last December, he pulled out of plans to build a Trump Tower in Belgrade, at the site of the former Yugoslav defense ministry, which was bombed by NATO in 1999. His investment firm withdrew from the project hours after the country’s organized-crime prosecutors indicted the culture minister for allegedly forging documents as part of the process to remove the building’s cultural-heritage status. The company issued a statement saying the plans would not proceed, because “meaningful projects should unite rather than divide.”

In Albania, people involved in the resort project maintain that construction isn’t yet under way, even though environmental activists say fencing has gone up, trees have been removed, and sand dunes have been flattened. The country amended its environmental regulations in 2024, cutting a path for the development by lifting construction bans in protected ecological zones. The move drew a warning from the European Parliament about compliance with environmental standards as part of Albania’s bid to join the bloc. Rama’s office didn’t respond to a request for comment, but Asher Abehsera, one of the Trump couple’s partners in the endeavor, told me in a statement, “Our goal is simple: celebrate Albania’s natural beauty, create jobs, and build something future generations can be proud of.”

He added: “Its future will ultimately be determined by Albania and the Albanian people.”

Large-scale demonstrations began in Albania at the end of May, after video circulated of private security guards punching and dragging a protester along a beach in Zvërnec, the coastal enclave where part of the Trump-family project is planned. “This was the tipping point,” Ditmar Bushati, a former Albanian foreign minister, told me. “It started as a dispute over access to the sea and access to nature, and it was transformed in a couple of days into a protest about who should decide the future of the country.”

Thousands mobilized in Tirana, the capital, with signs proclaiming that the nation was not for sale and declaring, I don’t want Albania like Dubai. Rama also dug in his heels, issuing a statement saying, “There is absolutely no chance that the investment will stop ​as long as I am here.” As the protests grew, they became a platform for frenzied speculation and conspiracy theories. Some protesters saw parallels to Kushner’s support for a proposed “Gaza Riviera,” fearing that Albanian land would be used to resettle Palestinians. Further afield, online commenters called the development “another Epstein island,” a reference to the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein’s hideouts in the U.S. Virgin Islands. A separate controversy, over government-backed plans for a Kanye West concert outside Tirana, added to the grievances. Attention came from all ideological corners. Senator Bernie Sanders welcomed the protests as a dissent against “global oligarchy.” Alex Jones, the right-wing conspiracy theorist, enthused about an impending “civil war.”

The prime minister argued that the protests had been hijacked by foreign actors, blaming “Trump haters” as well as “enemies of Israel and Albania.” Albania, where about half of the population is Muslim, has strong ties to Israel rooted in historical goodwill. During the Second World War, Albanians motivated by an ancient honor code called besa, literally “to keep the promise,” hid thousands of Jewish refugees; it was the only European country with more Jewish residents after the Holocaust than before. In this century, Albania made itself a target of the Iranian regime when it agreed, at the request of the United States and the United Nations in 2013, to host exiled members of the dissident group Mujahedin-e-Khalq, or MEK. In 2022, Albania expelled Iranian diplomats after concluding that Tehran was behind a wave of cyberattacks aimed at government institutions and critical infrastructure. In recent weeks, Rama has accused Iran of waging a “hybrid war” by spreading disinformation about the development and inflaming opposition to his government’s plans. Iran has denied the accusations.

Protesters appear intent on proving that the uprising is authentic and homegrown. Bushati, the former foreign minister, has celebrated the protests as “the best investment in our image as a people and in our path towards the European Union.” He said as many as 200,000 people mobilized over the July 4 weekend and dismissed claims of foreign interference. “Iran has zero influence in Albania,” he said. “This is a country living happily in the U.S. orbit.”

Iranian influence hardly seems necessary to destabilize the resort plans. The land on which part of the project is planned is contested. Sazan, the island, is owned by the state, so a lease would ultimately be necessary; the Trump-linked consortium currently has what’s called “strategic investor” status, which helps accelerate permitting and negotiations for a long-term agreement. The coastal land in Zvërnec, however, is private. Entities linked to the Trump couple purchased it from a Miami-based businessman, Artur Shehu, who insists that his family’s claim on the land dates to Ottoman times. Locals in Zvërnec argue that they are the rightful owners. It’s a messy picture because land was requisitioned by the state during the Communist period; when properties returned to private hands in the 1990s, ownership was often disputed.

The saga became considerably more dramatic this summer, when an Albanian agency devoted to countering financial crimes, the Special Structure Against Corruption and Organized Crime, said it was investigating whether Shehu had forged deeds to the property. Finding out whether he has lawful title to the land is just one part of a broader probe by the agency into allegations that Shehu and his associates had trafficked cocaine into European ports and then laundered the money for real-estate projects. A message sent to a number associated with Shehu went unanswered; his attorney denied the allegations to Reuters. The Trump couple and their partners in the venture are not accused of wrongdoing. A spokesperson for the venture said, “The Albanian legal system is the appropriate venue for resolving questions about land ownership and the integrity of prior transactions. We respect that process, and as always, we will cooperate with any lawful process as required.”

The Flamingo Revolution reflects dissatisfaction with Albania’s democratic institutions 35 years after the fall of Communism. Yet the popular mobilization, combined with the anti-corruption inquiry that seeks to validate some of its core claims, is evidence of democratic maturity. Albanian society has developed antibodies to signs of state capture. The irony is that an initiative emanating from the American president’s family would trigger such a powerful immune response.

entries.title: How the American Office Became Inevitable
entries.summary: In the 19th century, “artisans of the brain” flooded to office jobs to prove themselves.
entries.content:
In the 19th century, “artisans of the brain” flooded to office jobs to prove themselves.

This is an edition of Time-Travel Thursdays, a journey through The Atlantic’s archives to contextualize the present. Sign up here.

One of the most gruesome murders in 19th-century America happened in a New York City office, just across the street from city hall. In 1841, Samuel Adams, a printer of no apparent relation to the famous Revolutionary, paid a visit to John Colt, the author of a popular bookkeeping manual and brother to a famous gunmaker. Adams had gone to see Colt about the $1.35 he believed he was due. In the argument that ensued, it’s not clear why Colt reached for a hatchet—he claimed self-defense—but he did, and he used it to bludgeon Adams to death. To hide the deed, Colt cleaned up the blood, stripped the corpse, and stuffed it into a large wooden crate in the room. The next day, he had the crate loaded onto a ship bound for New Orleans. The stinking body was soon discovered in the hold, and Colt was arrested not long after that.

Twelve years later, in 1853, Herman Melville was still thinking about the murder. Curiously, his sympathies rested with the killer—the man he called “the hapless Colt” in his great tale of office work and alienation, “Bartleby, the Scrivener.” The story’s narrator theorized that the crime would not have happened if the men had met anywhere but in an office. It was that setting, “entirely unhallowed by humanizing domestic associations,” that drove Colt to such “irritable desperation.”

What is it about the office? From its beginnings, it’s been a place of anxiety and conflict, if not usually of the murderous variety. It was, in Melville’s time, a novel space born out of the growing scale of capitalism. Industrial production, steam-powered transportation, and ever more trade required a new realm of work managing and tracking the massive volumes of information being produced. Young people, mostly men from the countryside, flooded into cities to meet the demand, and perhaps to make something of themselves. They kept accounts, tracked commodities, copied correspondence, and recorded legal documents. In the process, they complained a good deal about this tedious and odd kind of work, even as it became a pillar of modern economic life.

In the 19th century, “clerks” quickly became a sizable portion of the urban workforce (already the third-largest category of laborers in New York by 1855). Writing in The Atlantic in 1861, Thomas Wentworth Higginson explained the attendant shift in human labor concisely: “All hand-work is constantly being transmuted into brain-work.” In a world that had long equated labor with the physical act of making things, such a shift was seen as not terribly manly, and potentially dangerous to society as a whole. In an essay endorsing a robust program of “gymnastics” for clerks, Higginson lamented that these “dandies of the desk,” these “artisans of the brain,” would wither away, creating a nation of weaklings.

But the problem went beyond the body. Repetitive mental work in such unhallowed spaces also punished the mind and the soul. After sitting all day, Higginson said, the average clerk “leaves his place of business at nightfall with his head a mere furnace of red-hot brains and his body a pile of burnt-out cinders.” Not everyone went slowly insane under such circumstances, as Bartleby does in Melville’s story, but the experience was disorienting all the same. The misery of what Higginson described as “over-writing one’s self” was not just a literary idea; as the scholar Thomas Augst has shown, the diaries of this first generation of clerks record a similar sense of self-negation. “Everything is at its highest pitch of dullness. No life in anything, but all seems as inanimate as lifeless matter,” one entry states.

The inefficiencies of early office work drove developments in information technology and modern management practices. Adding machines, cash registers, mimeographs, punch cards, and eventually computers maximized productivity while creating stricter office systems and carefully prescribed processes. Beginning in the late 19th century, the office also produced a new gender division of labor, as more of the repetitive work—typing, filing, stenography—fell to a secretarial class of women with limited prospects for advancement.

The quiet struggles of the office never quite went away. The modern capitalist economy continued to expand, and the machines and systems developed to manage it all didn’t reduce the work: Generating and processing more information seemed to only generate more information to process. In Sinclair Lewis’s 1917 novel The Job, the heroine, Una, sees it all quite plainly. “She knew that the machines were supposed to save work,” Lewis wrote. “But she was aware that the girls worked just as hard and long and hopelessly after their introduction as before.”

Although many workers chafed against the confines of office life, demand for these jobs only grew. For men, what we now call white-collar work has become the route to social mobility, affluence, and a measure of status. For women, despite the exploitation and the very real limits on advancement they have faced, office work has offered them more financial independence and public standing than ever available before. Today, the white-collar workplace is often seen as the most reliable pathway to middle-class comfort and standing.

This model of progress was evident even in the games played at the time. In 1889, Parker Brothers released the board game Office Boy, which had a simple premise: You began on the margins, with one small act of initiative—Apply for Situation as Office Boy—and moved toward the center, Head of the Firm. Positive qualities—promptness, diligence, cheerfulness—sped up your ascent through the ranks; negative ones—laziness, dissipation, late mornings—slowed it down. The lesson was plain. Your future was in your hands, and with a little bit of luck and pluck and a good roll of the dice, you just might be the boss.

When you buy a book using a link in this newsletter, we receive a commission. Thank you for supporting The Atlantic.

entries.title: The Fear of Missing The Odyssey
entries.summary: The new blockbuster’s box-office success affirms the power of spectacle.
entries.content:
The new blockbuster’s box-office success affirms the power of spectacle.

Christopher Nolan shouldn’t have many personal box-office records left to break. He’s had a long career involving a slew of hits, critical and commercial, including Oscar wins, a viral theatergoing campaign, and a wildly successful Batman trilogy. But leveling up is exactly what he did with The Odyssey last weekend, when his latest blockbuster opened to $264.1 million worldwide—more than his Best Picture winner Oppenheimer started with in 2023, and more than any other R-rated theatrical release this year. Nolan is a proven quantity at this point, but Hollywood still has every reason to cheer such a gargantuan start for an adaptation of a centuries-old poem.

The Odyssey’s haul thus far is a welcome sign for Hollywood. The movie now seems likely to bring in more than $400 million in North America, which would make it only the second R-rated film to ever do so. The achievement is the most recent example of what’s been working for cinema of late: a sense of originality. The Odyssey tweaks Homer’s classic story into a moodier version of the swords-and-sandals epic—and represents a rare swerve away from tired sequels, cheap remakes, and creaky cinematic universes. More pointed, however, its achievements offer two major lessons for the industry to draw from: Bet on quality, and don’t shy away from spectacle.

After all, The Odyssey’s initial success has both a highbrow and a lowbrow element to it. The Odyssey benefits from its august source material and auteur-branded advertising, the appeal of which has only grown in the years since “Barbenheimer” powered Oppenheimer to a nearly $1 billion worldwide gross. But on top of that, it has been marketed as a big-screen experience that audiences simply cannot skip. The Odyssey was the first movie shot entirely in IMAX’s large, nearly square aspect ratio, meaning viewers literally miss out on seeing some of the image when the film is projected on a regular screen. Ticket sales for IMAX screenings accounted for more than 20 percent of the opening gross around the world, representing a growing trend for blockbusters; 53 percent of the domestic total came from all “premium large formats.” The technical details behind the filmmaking appeal to cinephiles who appreciate the nitty-gritty of the production, while that hype also trickles down to those less in the know—audiences, perhaps not wanting to wait for a streaming release, are packing it in at regular auditoriums too. And as they keep seeking out the most impressive way to watch the film, audiences will continue to reinforce the idea that The Odyssey is a major theatrical event.

That formula worked for Amazon MGM’s sci-fi dramedy Project Hail Mary earlier this year, another heartening success that was billed as a visual spectacle filmed specifically for IMAX. Scale isn’t the only factor that compels audiences, however; A24 and Focus Features, the studios behind the horror phenomenons Backrooms and Obsession, held off on streaming releases, as viewers stateside continued to prioritize the theatrical experience. Meanwhile, 2026’s most notable failures have followed rulebooks that seem less and less valuable. The first new Star Wars movie in seven years, The Mandalorian and Grogu, had the saga’s lowest-grossing opening weekend ever. Supergirl, DC Comics’ continuation of a new superhero universe that was sparked with last year’s successful Superman, crashed and burned. Most shocking, Disney’s critically panned live-action remake of Moana will likely lose the studio at least $100 million, two years after the animated sequel Moana 2 made more than $1 billion worldwide.

Theatergoers are more frequently rejecting retreads of stretched-thin intellectual property, released by studios that seem afraid of taking the slightest gamble. Industry analysts could have warned Disney that there was little value in remaking Moana, a beloved classic that is nonetheless not quite 10 years old. Yet audiences had been mostly rewarding these efforts until now, even if reviews were poor or word of mouth was tepid. This year seems to be the culmination of a growing shift by viewers away from franchise tentpoles (though some series entries, such as the new Spider-Man and Avengers sequels, are expected to still punch through); moviegoers are seemingly moving instead toward efforts that feel more special. Imitating Christopher Nolan is something of a fool’s errand, of course, given that he makes one movie about every three years and tends to be pretty painstaking about it. But investing in and celebrating filmmakers’ biggest visions—and the grandeur of the visuals they’re creating—is proving again and again to be a risk worth taking.

entries.title: Is the Age of Reading Over?
entries.summary: Rose Horowitch on her cover story and what happens when a society becomes “postliterate”
entries.content:
Rose Horowitch on her cover story and what happens when a society becomes “postliterate”

Subscribe here: Apple Podcasts | Spotify | YouTube | Overcast | Pocket Casts

Over the past 18 months, scholars have debated—and likely will continue to long into the future—why voters returned Donald Trump to the presidency. One intriguing theory: It’s because Americans don’t read books anymore. Fewer than half of all adults reported reading a book of any kind in 2022; only 38 percent read a novel or short story. And the proportion of Americans reading for pleasure on any given day is plummeting, according to the latest Atlantic cover story, “The End of Reading Is Here,” by Rose Horowitch.



We are all awash in text, of course: messages from friends, headlines, captions on videos. But not the kinds of rich, layered sentences that have shaped our brains for centuries and given rise to many academic disciplines. Hence, we are a populace susceptible to a man who can capture attention, stir emotion, and breezily contradict himself, because his words are not etched in stone, or anywhere, really.

Horowitch joins Radio Atlantic to discuss what it means to live in a postliterate society, what we’ve lost, and how we might recover.

The following is a transcript of the episode:

Hanna Rosin: We’re going to have a conversation about the end of reading today. This idea, proposed in the most recent Atlantic cover story, is that the age of reading is turning out to be a short anomaly in human history and that we are fast entering a postliterate society.

But before we do that, I want you to think about the arc of your own relationship with books. How did you read as a kid? In school? When did that change? Did you notice it change? What’s the last book you read, absorbed, and enjoyed?

[Music]

Rosin: I’m Hanna Rosin. This is Radio Atlantic.

I’m guessing that you’ve heard someone lament about how students—in middle school, high school, or even in college—don’t read whole books anymore. Maybe you have a teacher friend who complains about having to assign excerpts or increasingly simple texts. Maybe you are a young student, and books seem annoying and time-consuming to you. Or maybe you’re an adult and just find yourself trying to read but instead reaching for your phone every couple of pages.

The argument we’re discussing today is much broader than mere phone distraction and spans back to ancient Egypt. It’s about how reading shaped the human mind and then human society, and how that’s now veering in a different direction.

Rosin: Do you think more people actually read your full article or heard about the debate on TikTok?

Rose Horowitch: I hope that people read it, but I don’t know.

Rosin: I’m talking today to the author of that cover story, Rose Horowitch, who is not the elderly professor type that you might normally assume wrote an Atlantic story called “The End of Reading Is Here.”

Horowitch: It’s so funny because I initially had a joke in there, like, You probably think I’m retirement age, reading this whole piece.

Rosin: Rose is actually in her 20s.

Horowitch: Yes.

Rosin: She did grow up learning to love to read. But then as she got older, she found it harder.

Horowitch: That’s a story probably a lot of people can relate to, of reading much more than when you were young and then gradually noticing it disappear.

I think I was noticing more so when I would try to read a book that I would just, after every couple pages, want to check my phone in case I missed a notification. Or I would take a look at my watch to make sure that I didn’t have any notifications. So I had to start putting my phone in drawers or putting it in another room so that I really could not check it. So that was more what I noticed, yeah.

Rosin: Right. So that’s your experience, and from your article, I’m seeing that’s the experience of lots of people. So just lay out the basics. In what ways and among whom has reading books declined?

Horowitch: So reading books has declined very broadly, even among the groups of people that, you know, have been the most stalwart readers: Women and retirees and college graduates, they’re seeing a decline. We know that in the past two decades, the share of Americans that read a book or an article on any given day has declined by 40 percent.

And that, as of 2022, which is the last year that we have really great data for, fewer than half of all Americans read a book at all in the year, and only 38 percent read a novel or short story. So we know that reading is just really in decline kind of across demographic groups.

Rosin: And is it the types of books people now read, has that changed as well? I’m feeling very superior now ’cause I just finished my book last night, but anyway, keep going. (Laughs.)

Horowitch: (Laughs.)

Rosin: Yeah.

Horowitch: So the types of books—we know that when people do read, the books that they’re reading are shorter and simpler than they used to be. New York Times best sellers have sentences that are about one-third shorter than they were a century ago. And I spoke with the head of the New York Public Library, and he was saying that young-adult fiction has really become one of their most popular offerings, even among people who are not young adults. So we’re definitely seeing that when people do pick up a book, the book that they read is shorter and the sentences are simpler and the vocabulary’s simpler, and that’s a real change as well.

Rosin: You’ve written a lot about the decline of reading. I feel like it’s your subbeat now. (Laughs.)

Horowitch: Yes.

Rosin: When you were doing research for this particular story, was there statistics or a comment from a teacher or something that really stuck with you or surprised you?

Horowitch: I think some of the things that have been most chilling for me in reporting this out were the things that were really about children reading. And so we know that only 2 percent of American adults read to a child on any given day. And we do see that kind of every year older a child gets, the less they like to read.

I spoke with one researcher who did focus groups, and high-school students described reading for pleasure as an alien practice. And so that really stuck out in my mind, just like, Okay, how did we get to this point, when kids view reading this way?

Rosin: The way this argument is framed—and you just used the word chilling—is: There’s a problem. Who should we blame for the problem? Or a lot of people will read the article that way, and some will immediately go to Kids today, they’re always on their phones, and this generation of students. Some will say schools just gave up, and it’s this iteration of education that’s the problem.

Do you, in your head, have a clear answer for who to blame?

Horowitch: Well, it’s difficult to know exactly how much each factor plays into it. I definitely don’t think that it’s just kids these days.

Rosin: That’s why I’m glad you wrote this story—

Horowitch: Right.

Rosin: —and not the retiring professor. Right.

Horowitch: Yes. As a member of Gen Z, I’m like, If it’s kids these days, it’s me. But I think this is a story of technologies crowding out the time that people would’ve once spent reading books. We know that when the television was first invented, American households were spending tons of time watching that. Then obviously streaming, internet, smartphone, social media—it all just takes up so much of our time now that might have previously been devoted to reading a book.

Rosin: We’re in some ways inundated with text. I feel on a daily basis I read a much higher quantity of actual words than I used to. So what is the difference?

Horowitch: Yes, exactly. So people often talk about this as a literacy crisis, and I don’t think that that’s right at all. Our literacy rate is historically very high. We are probably reading more words than ever with social media, the number of emails you get for your job, and text messages with friends and everything.

And so the difference is that we’re almost swimming in so much text that we just don’t actually have the time or, sort of, attention to kind of focus for an extended period on a rich or complex work, because our attention is turning to so many different things. And so that’s why I called it really a postliterate age instead of an illiterate age.

Rosin: Explain postliterate. That’s a really important word in your argument. What does postliterate mean?

Horowitch: Right. So what I was trying to get across is that reading is not just one mode of communication or one skill among many. When reading and writing first appeared about 6,000 years ago, it transformed society, and it changed the way people thought and politics and culture. And so I was trying to get across that I think that we’re living through a civilizational shift on that scale, where we’re gonna see changes in our consciousness, changes in the candidates we elect, and what our culture values, and even how we tell the story of our civilization.

So that was what I was trying to get across by postliterate, that we can still read, but it’s an age where text is just not the kind of medium that we use to spread information or knowledge or culture or entertainment, and that that will have really far-reaching effects.

Rosin: That was an excellent and fast summary, but I also feel like one thing about this argument is: It can seem like an elite argument that you and I and the people who read The Atlantic care about. It’s sort of ironic that you write—I don’t know how many words it was—a 7,000-word, very-well-written piece to explain how your generation doesn’t really read or have an attention span anymore.

So I actually wanna dig in a little more slowly and deeply to why reading is important, ’cause we can summarize it quickly and just say to people reading is important. But I think until you actually understand the shifts that happened in human evolution or our brain via reading, it doesn’t seem that important. I can be like, I read what I want. Leave me alone.

So maybe we’ll start with the work of Walter Ong.

Walter Ong: Now, I understand that my task is to try to talk to you. Would you mind if I took my jacket off? It’s a little bit warmer in here—

Rosin: I love his work because he’s a priest, so everything he writes about and thinks about has a spiritual dimension. He was also a teacher. So talk about his work around oral traditions versus written traditions and how they change the brain.

Horowitch: Right. So I think the one thing that Ong really understood was that writing text is just a very different process than speaking it.

Ong: Writing has reinforced the old. It’s made talkers talk more, but it’s made them talk differently.

[Music]

Horowitch: We tend to use more complex sentence structures and vocabulary. When you write something down, it gives you distance because you can see it reflected back at you. So you can go back to it and get new understanding. You can kind of take on a more complex analytical task. It’s a more dispassionate spread of information because you don’t immediately see the person who wrote it at the same time.

Ong: It was the first time it ever occurred to him that every one of those definitions in the dictionary was something that somebody said this word means. The word didn’t come equipped with it. Somebody had to figure that out and say, This is what it means.

Horowitch: And so he was arguing that it’s just very different and that this ushers in a sort of entirely new mode of thinking, a kind of rational, linear, analytical way of thinking. And that that is a precondition for a lot of the modern types of study that we think about today. Philosophy and modern science and history as an academic discipline, that those things required reading and writing.

Ong: They can learn facts, but they cannot learn how to think in the way in which you have to think. The thought patterns that you have to be able to use cannot be mastered without writing.

Rosin: Yeah, there’s this quote you reference from Amusing Ourselves to Death, the 1985 Neil Postman book: “Writing freezes speech.” And I think what he meant by that is that speech happens fluidly, but writing inserts a pause; and that pause allows us to reflect, shape our thoughts; and all this metacognition is happening when you write that doesn’t necessarily happen in speech, because speech is constantly moving. Is it something like that? Because I never actually thought in detail about what reading does to your brain versus writing.

Horowitch: Yeah, so I think there’s two elements to it. One is that we just speak a lot faster than we write. And so it forces you to slow down and think a little bit more about what you’re putting out there. And then at the same time, when it is frozen before you, you can see it reflected back at you, this message, and so you can return to it for new understanding, think about how to reorder it, edit it, all these things that we can’t really do with speech, ’cause it just disappears into the ether.

And so it sort of allows for this kind of logical way of thinking, and that, yeah, that quote from Postman really stuck out to me too.

Rosin: I’m just realizing that podcasting, therefore, is for Neanderthals because— (Laughs.)

Horowitch: No. No, because it’s secondary orality. It’s mediated through this kind of writing, ’cause we’re talking about writing.

Rosin: Okay. Secondary orality. I’ll take it.

Okay, so that’s the long period that we’ve been through now, where we’ve evolved our brains, evolved a lot of human intellectual disciplines via reading, and now we’re entering towards postliteracy. What are the characteristics of a postliterate society, as opposed to the literate society you just explained?

Horowitch: Yeah, so as part of my reporting for this piece, I interviewed a lot of cognitive scientists and was kind of trying to understand, Okay, what does happen to our consciousness? And I think what we see is that we know from neuroscience that what you practice is what your brain gets good at. And so as we’re reading less, we’re going to have less background knowledge to aid in comprehension. We’re going to have a less rational, analytical mode of thinking.

We’re more accustomed to switching our attention and less accustomed to a kind of linear, extended focus on something. Even two decades ago, the average attention span on a screen was two-and-a-half minutes. And five years ago, it was 47 seconds.

Rosin: There are some times when you describe it dispassionately, it almost sounds like a superpower, you know? Because speed is often thought of as good. We test students on how quickly they can do things. Tests are always timed. So I don’t know, just as you were talking, I was thinking, Could speed be some kind of intellectual superpower that we just don’t understand yet?

Horowitch: Right. Well, I was trying to leave open that possibility. We do see, even as we see on tests, that it appears that IQs have been declining for the past two decades. At the same time, we are doing better on some forms of spatial rotation. So I definitely wanted to leave open the possibility that there could be these cognitive benefits that we just don’t understand and that this is a trade-off, but what we get will end up being worth it. And I think it’s too soon to tell if that’s possible.

[Music]

Rosin: After the break, who might thrive in a postliterate society? A clue: He’s currently president.

[Break]

Rosin: Okay, so that’s on the level of, like, an individual brain. There’s also an entire civilization because you start—

Horowitch: (Laughs.)

Rosin: (Laughs.) —the story at a broad civilization level. We can start at the easiest place, is with Trump. You describe him as the ideal president for a postliterate society. What does that mean?

Horowitch: Yeah, so what I meant by that was that it’s sort of difficult to imagine Trump being elected in a country where information is primarily spread through text. But he really understands how to kind of grab attention and keep it in this postliterate age.

So we see that he actually has kind of retrieved a lot of conventions from preliterate times. He contradicts himself as though there’s no record of what he said before.

[Music]

President Trump (in May): You’ll never have a leak. It’s very strong. If you had a knife, you can’t even cut it, it’s so strong, so powerful. It’s like powerful rubber.

Reporter (in June): National Guard and police have been all over the Mall. How would these vandals have gotten so close to do something like that?

Trump (in June): We didn’t have a lot of them then. Who would think that somebody would go into a pool and take a knife and start cutting it?

Horowitch: He speaks with a lot of emotion, you know, often anger, and that’s just easier to remember than when someone gives a sort of dispassionate speech.

Trump: I think it’s a disgrace. And I say that, and I say that, and that’s something that Nazi Germany would have done and did do. I think it’s a disgrace.

Horowitch: We also see that he uses these kind of epithets that almost call back to Homer in the way that the poet described people. He says, “Sleepy Joe,” “Crooked Hillary,” and that’s just way easier to remember and repeat. And you just start to associate it.

Trump: I call him Crooked Joe and Sleepy Joe. I find them to be equal actually. I do polls. Which is better: Crooked Joe? Or Sleepy Joe? And they poll about even.

Horowitch: So we see that Trump has kind of understood how to entertain people, go viral, present things as very simple and authentic, and that’s helped him get elected.

Rosin: The piece you wrote caused quite a stir, and especially among people who love books.

People who love books tend to read The Atlantic, so maybe we’re getting a skewed sample. But let’s just say it caused quite a stir in the book-reading public, especially the publishing world. Is there any pushback that you’ve received that you think is interesting?

Horowitch: Well, I think it’s interesting—there are a lot of positive signs for reading, which I did try to indicate in the story. Print-book sales were higher last year than they were a decade ago, and so that is certainly a fair critique.

But at the same time, I tried to indicate in the piece, when I noted that, that what we’re seeing is that the people who are devoted to text are perhaps even more devoted to it than ever before. They spend more time reading than they used to, but they’re just becoming a smaller and smaller minority in society.

Rosin: One thing I was thinking about is the democratization of text. When you wrote about people reading shorter sentences or YA novels, maybe one way of looking at this is that there used to be gatekeepers, people who would tell us what was literary, what was considered good to read, whereas now maybe you read what you want, which might be Colleen Hoover, which might be fan fiction, which might be someone’s online manifesto. And is that necessarily bad in the same way everything’s been democratized? Because if more people are buying books, it just means maybe they’re buying not literary books, but books.

Horowitch: No, and that—I think that is a fair critique. And it sort of fits in with audiobooks almost, that they make it more accessible to read. And so I definitely do think it is a good thing if stories and information has been democratized and are accessible to more people. I think the challenge is just: Even as these things have become more widely available, we are still seeing a huge decline in reading. So it’s like, have people taken advantage of them? It’s not clear to me.

Rosin: Yeah. And is your worry that because you write so much about reading inside the educational system, that down the road people will even stop reading those kinds of books because reading is just so much less fluidly integrated into education at all levels?

Horowitch: Definitely we see in schools that teachers are just assigning far fewer books. I think a survey from last year found that most middle- and high-school teachers assign between zero and four books a year. And so we do see a big change where there’s a lot more emphasis on excerpts and on reading-comprehension drills.

I guess my concern would be: You want everyone to have the option to read any work that they would want to, and so just making sure that students are equipped with the skills and mode of attention and practice that they can read whatever they want to instead of having some of it be unavailable to them.

Rosin: Right. And I suppose there’s also a return to the analog, like reading retreats and that kind of thing. So there’s some kind of self-conscious niche, like I’m a reader. So it’s almost like your identity is you are a reader, and you go out to a reading club, and that’s who you are. It’s not everybody. It’s not a sign of a literate society. But it is your people.

Horowitch: Yeah. One thing that really struck me in that Postman book was that before radio and television was invented, there was no way to be a reader, because there was no way to not be a reader. If you knew how to read, you had to. That was the only way to get information.

And so it’s only now that being a reader is optional, that it really can be an identity marker. And we are seeing with some young people that there really is this drive towards more analog technologies. It seems to be a backlash to living in a digital age where everything feels ephemeral.

I spoke with the head of the Cleveland Public Library, and he was saying that the young people that come in, they want a print book because they want something that feels tangible and concrete and that they can hold. So there certainly is that countervailing sign.

Rosin: I know. It’s so funny. It’s like a print book is like a vinyl record.

Horowitch: (Laughs.)

Rosin: (Laughs.) Like, there will be stores that are like, We sell books and vinyl records.

The bombshell of AI: One thing I noticed is that AI has an oddly formal way of speaking to you. Like, it’s a lot more like the letters you quoted from the average Civil War soldier than it is like texting. And so I know that, superficially, it seems like, Of course AI will destroy deep thinking and reading, but I also sense that AI returns a level of kind of full-sentences formality. Like, Here is how you should think through things. There is a kind of a return to discipline and what it’s presenting to you in a way that I find interesting.

Horowitch: No, it’s funny. It’s sort of the ultimate irony that we talk about this technology that’s just gonna destroy thinking or whatever, and it’s text or, at least, now it communicates with us via text. And so the point that I tried to make is that we are actually living in a time where there’s more text.

AI has led to a threefold increase in the number of books released on Amazon, more scientific papers being published. And a lot of this text at first brush appears polished and professional and something that you might trust and take in. And so that’s why I think it’s more important than ever to cultivate your powers of critical thinking and discernment, because AI is creating this superabundance of text, and we need to be able to sift through it.

Rosin: Maybe that’s a useful way to think about this argument, that it’s not just about books. It’s not just about novels. It is about taking note of a world in which, because you are inundated with text, you can have this feeling that you are, in fact, reading a lot, but maybe pay attention to the ways in which whatever reading you’re doing is eroding an ability to think critically, think deeply, think independently. Just note that evolution or change.

[Music]

Horowitch: No, and I think that that has been true for so long. But one thing that I think we might be seeing now is that for a while at least, a lot of information on social media was presented in text. And now we sort of see with new capabilities just so much more short-form video.

TikToker 1: The United States of America is becoming postliterate.

TikToker 2: The end of reading is here, so I wanna talk about why this is a reality that I refuse to accept. Over the weekend, I read The Atlantic’s August cover story, about the death of reading.

TikToker 3: According to the National Endowment for the Arts, fewer than half of American adults reported having read any book in 2022.

Horowitch: So I do wonder if we will get to a point where we’re not even reading more text, because it’s just videos now.

TikToker 4: I’m about to talk about the death of reading on the app that is allegedly killing it. Stay with me.

Rosin: Yeah, there was a really interesting interview with one of the leading figures at The New York Times who talked about the Times shifting very explicitly towards video, and that seemed like a moment. Partly just because we’re journalists, but we do think of The New York Times as a bastion of sort of literary, straight newsgathering. So it’s something interesting. And them acknowledging the future is video, we haven’t really even talked about that. We’ve been pretending as if we’re going to continue to live in a world inundated with text, but that may not be true at all.

I know you interviewed a lot of professors, researchers. Did they have ideas beyond, Woe is me. I wish more people would read books? Do they have method strategies, anything they employ that wasn’t just kind of, Do like we used to do?

Horowitch: Well, I think there are some signs that there’s a little bit of a backlash brewing. Certainly, we see it against tech in schools, you know, states banning cellphones during the school day. And when Texas did so, a district in Dallas saw 200,000 more library books checked out. So I think that—

Rosin: Wait—explain the experiment again. What exactly happened?
